target.name: Arbe Robotics
target.founder: Kobi Marenko, Noam Arkind, Oz Fixman

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Joby Aviation
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Public), post-money $10B+

private.name: Archer Aviation
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Public), post-money $5B

data.narrative:
Arbe Robotics is a public-stage company in the Drones & Autonomous sector that has raised $118M to date. The valuation universe for this company is constrained: while six private peers operate in the sector—including Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation, Nuro, Elroy Air, and Aurora Innovation—there are no public comparables in this narrowly defined space. This composition creates a methodological challenge for traditional peer benchmarking.

The peer-median approach was not applicable to this analysis. The Drones & Autonomous sector contains only four same-sector peers total, but filtering by stage alignment, adjacent-stage proximity, or valuation tier yielded fewer than three datapoints in any narrow cohort. Applying an all-sector median would introduce significant noise and misalignment with Arbe's specific market and technology profile, rendering the result unreliable for valuation support.

The insufficient peer set—zero public comparables and a fragmented private landscape—means traditional triangulation methods cannot generate a defensible valuation range for Arbe Robotics at this time. Additional comparable data, either from recent funding rounds in the peer set or from public market transactions in adjacent autonomous systems segments, would be necessary to establish quantitative anchors. The primary valuation uncertainty stems from the nascency and heterogeneity of the sector itself, not from Arbe-specific factors.
